title: A toric case of the Thomas-Yau conjecture
speaker: Jacopo Stoppa
abstract: We consider a class of Lagrangian sections L contained in certain Calabi-Yau Lagrangian fibrations (mirrors of toric weak Fano manifolds). We prove that a form of the Thomas-Yau conjecture holds in this case: L is Hamiltonian isotopic to a special Lagrangian section in this class if and only if a stability condition holds, in the sense of a slope inequality on objects in a set of exact triangles in the Fukaya-Seidel category. This agrees with general proposals by Li. Under more restrictive assumptions, this result can be used to show a precise relation with Bridgeland stability, as predicted by Joyce. Based on arXiv:2505.07228.

proof_stages.label: Almost Calabi-Yau special Lagrangian problem
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up almost Calabi-Yau geometry, special Lagrangian submanifolds, their calibration/mean-curvature-flow and physics motivations, and the Thomas-Yau expectation that solvability should be a stability condition rather than merely a Hamiltonian-isotopy problem.

proof_stages.label: Categorical stability templates
proof_stages.summary: Yang Li's exact-triangle phase inequalities and Joyce's Bridgeland-stability conjectures are presented as the precise categorical models for relating periods of the holomorphic volume form to existence of special Lagrangians, with weak and singular objects expected in general.

proof_stages.label: Toric SYZ mirror model and admissible sections
proof_stages.summary: For a projective toric variety X, the SYZ mirror is described as an almost Calabi-Yau domain in an algebraic torus with Landau-Ginzburg potential W, and Lagrangian sections of the mirror torus fibration are identified with Hermitian metrics on holomorphic line bundles on X.

proof_stages.label: Finite-triangle toric Thomas-Yau theorem
proof_stages.summary: Under weak Fano hypotheses and positivity of the inverse line bundle, the main theorem asserts that a special Lagrangian section exists exactly when finitely many phase inequalities, attached to exact triangles indexed by toric subvarieties, hold in the supercritical phase range.

proof_stages.label: Analytic reduction to dHYM and supercritical existence
proof_stages.summary: The special Lagrangian equation for the mirror section is transformed into the deformed Hermitian-Yang-Mills/constant Lagrangian phase equation on X, whose supercritical solvability is characterized by finitely many Nakai-Moishezon-type positivity integrals and yields a unique smooth solution.

proof_stages.label: Algebraic central-charge recoding
proof_stages.summary: The analytic positivity criterion is reinterpreted inside Db(X) by constructing objects whose algebraic central charges have phase inequalities equivalent, after a genericity assumption to avoid equalities, to the analytic inequalities.

proof_stages.label: Mirror and Gamma-theorem comparison to periods
proof_stages.summary: Homological mirror symmetry transfers the Db(X) objects to the Fukaya-Seidel category, and the Gamma theorem plus a large-volume/large-Lagrangian limit identifies the relevant algebraic central-charge inequalities with period inequalities of the holomorphic volume form, completing the Thomas-Yau form of the result.

proof_stages.label: Joyce-style Bridgeland stability refinements
proof_stages.summary: For toric weak Fano surfaces, and more restrictively for certain threefolds, existing Bridgeland stability conditions can be transported to the Fukaya-Seidel category so that stability of shifted Lagrangian sections implies solvability, with the converse depending on known or conjectural descriptions of destabilizing objects.

examples.name: Guillemin boundary-condition analogy
examples.note: In answering a question about Hamiltonian isotopy at infinity, the speaker compares admissible Lagrangian sections to Guillemin boundary conditions for toric Kähler metrics, making the hidden boundary behavior more concrete without driving the proof.
examples.engagement_reason: he only uses Guillemin boundary conditions as an analogy for the extension condition at the toric boundary

examples.name: Complex dimension two special case
examples.note: The speaker notes that in dimension two the supercritical phase restriction is essentially absent and the deformed Hermitian-Yang-Mills equation coincides with the complex Monge-Ampere equation, illustrating why the higher-dimensional analysis is harder.
examples.engagement_reason: he specializes the discussion to complex dimension two/surfaces, noting how the phase condition and dHYM equation simplify and formulating the resulting stability–solvability statement

passes.reason: Complex dimension two is the low-dimensional boundary case where the supercritical condition becomes vacuous and the equation simplifies.

examples.name: Toric del Pezzo surface cases
examples.note: The known cases of the Arcara-Miles conjecture, including blow-ups of the toric surface in one or two points and the trivial cases of P2 and P1 x P1, illustrate when the surface-level stability converse becomes unconditional.

examples.name: Torsion sheaf on a thickened toric boundary component
examples.note: In Q&A, a cone represented by a torsion coherent sheaf supported along the toric boundary is used to illustrate how the abstract Fukaya-Seidel objects may correspond to immersed or mildly singular Lagrangians.
examples.engagement_reason: in the Q&A he traces this torsion sheaf as the cone in a short exact sequence and discusses its mirror as a possibly immersed object supported on a thickened toric boundary component

passes.reason: A torsion sheaf on a thickened toric boundary component is rank-zero and lower-dimensional/nonreduced, while remaining torus-invariant.
A rank-zero torsion sheaf on a thickened toric boundary component is a lower-dimensional boundary-supported object used as an obstruction to a naive embedded-Lagrangian mirror picture.
A torsion sheaf on a nonreduced thickened boundary has collapsed lower-dimensional support and is invoked as an obstruction to representing all mirror objects by embedded Lagrangians.
A torsion sheaf on a thickened toric boundary component is rank-zero and non-locally-free on nonreduced lower-dimensional support, raised as a singular mirror object.
